content.text: Azure Architecture Center

The Azure Architecture Center (learn.microsoft.com/azure/architecture) is Microsoft's curated library of production-quality architectural guidance. It covers reference architectures, cloud design patterns, anti-patterns, Well-Architected Framework workload guidance, and best-practice articles — all vetted by Azure engineering teams and updated as services evolve.

attrs.alt: Azure Architecture Center content map — six content types and their focus areas

content.text: Reference Architectures

Reference architectures are end-to-end designs for specific workload types. Each includes an architecture diagram, component descriptions, scalability and availability considerations, a cost estimate, and — for most modern architectures — deployable Bicep or Terraform code.
Architecture
Workload Type
Key Services
AKS baseline
Container workloads — production baseline
AKS, Application Gateway, Key Vault, Container Registry, Azure Monitor
Multi-region web app
High-availability SaaS
Front Door, App Service, SQL Database geo-replication, Azure CDN
Hub-spoke network topology
Enterprise networking
Virtual WAN or VNet peering, Azure Firewall, ExpressRoute, VPN Gateway
Microservices with API Management
Service-oriented API platform
API Management, AKS, Service Bus, Event Grid, Cosmos DB
Serverless event processing
Event-driven real-time processing
Event Hubs, Azure Functions, Cosmos DB, Stream Analytics
AKS for regulated industries
Finance / healthcare compliance
AKS, Azure Policy, Defender for Containers, Private Link, Key Vault
SAP on Azure
ERP migration
VMs (M-series), HANA Large Instances, Proximity Placement Groups, NetApp Files
Deploying a Reference Architecture
Most Architecture Center designs ship with a companion GitHub repository containing Bicep or Terraform. The deployment follows a standard pattern: clone, set parameters, provision.

# Example: Deploy the AKS baseline architecture
git clone https://github.com/mspnp/aks-baseline.git
cd aks-baseline

# Review the required parameters
cat ./deploy.sh          # shows required environment variables
cat ./bicep/main.bicep   # root Bicep template

# Set required values
export LOCATION=eastus2
export RESOURCE_GROUP=rg-aks-baseline
export CLUSTER_NAME=aks-baseline-prod

# Provision infrastructure (Azure CLI + Bicep)
az group create --name $RESOURCE_GROUP --location $LOCATION

az deployment group create \
  --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --template-file ./bicep/main.bicep \
  --parameters \
    clusterName=$CLUSTER_NAME \
    location=$LOCATION \
    kubernetesVersion=1.29

# Verify the deployment
az aks show --name $CLUSTER_NAME --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --query "{state:provisioningState, k8sVersion:kubernetesVersion, nodes:agentPoolProfiles[0].count}" \
  --output table
Cloud Design Patterns

The Architecture Center documents 24 cloud design patterns — repeatable solutions to common distributed systems problems. Each pattern describes the problem, the solution structure, when to use it, and caveats.
Pattern
Problem Solved
Azure Implementation
Circuit Breaker
Prevent cascading failures when a downstream service degrades
Azure Functions with Polly, or APIM circuit breaker policy
CQRS
Separate read and write models for performance and scalability
Event Grid + Cosmos DB (write) + separate read replica or search index
Strangler Fig
Gradually migrate a monolith to microservices without big-bang rewrite
API Management routing old → new endpoints progressively
Saga
Maintain data consistency across microservices without distributed transactions
Azure Durable Functions orchestrator + compensating transactions
Competing Consumers
Scale message processing horizontally
Azure Service Bus queue + multiple Azure Functions consumers
Retry
Handle transient failures gracefully
Azure SDK built-in retry policies + exponential backoff
Throttling
Protect services from overload
API Management rate-limit policies, Azure Functions concurrency limits
Valet Key
Provide limited direct access to storage without proxying
Azure Blob SAS tokens with time-bound permissions
Implementing Circuit Breaker with Azure Functions

"""
Circuit breaker pattern for Azure Functions calling a downstream service.
Uses an Azure Table Storage cell to track failure state across invocations.
"""
import time, logging
from enum import Enum
from azure.data.tables import TableServiceClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

class CircuitState(Enum):
    CLOSED = "CLOSED"       # normal operation
    OPEN = "OPEN"           # blocking calls — downstream is down
    HALF_OPEN = "HALF_OPEN" # probing — one test call allowed

STORAGE_ACCOUNT = "https://yourstorage.table.core.windows.net"
TABLE_NAME = "circuitbreaker"

def get_circuit_state(service_name: str) -> dict:
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    client = TableServiceClient(endpoint=STORAGE_ACCOUNT, credential=cred)
    table = client.get_table_client(TABLE_NAME)
    try:
        entity = table.get_entity(partition_key="circuit", row_key=service_name)
        return {
            "state": entity.get("State", CircuitState.CLOSED.value),
            "failure_count": int(entity.get("FailureCount", 0)),
            "last_failure_time": float(entity.get("LastFailureTime", 0)),
        }
    except Exception:
        return {"state": CircuitState.CLOSED.value, "failure_count": 0, "last_failure_time": 0}

def record_failure(service_name: str, failure_threshold: int = 5) -> None:
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    client = TableServiceClient(endpoint=STORAGE_ACCOUNT, credential=cred)
    table = client.get_table_client(TABLE_NAME)
    state = get_circuit_state(service_name)
    new_count = state["failure_count"] + 1
    new_state = CircuitState.OPEN.value if new_count >= failure_threshold else state["state"]
    table.upsert_entity({
        "PartitionKey": "circuit",
        "RowKey": service_name,
        "State": new_state,
        "FailureCount": new_count,
        "LastFailureTime": time.time(),
    })

def record_success(service_name: str) -> None:
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    client = TableServiceClient(endpoint=STORAGE_ACCOUNT, credential=cred)
    table = client.get_table_client(TABLE_NAME)
    table.upsert_entity({
        "PartitionKey": "circuit",
        "RowKey": service_name,
        "State": CircuitState.CLOSED.value,
        "FailureCount": 0,
        "LastFailureTime": 0,
    })

def call_with_circuit_breaker(
    service_name: str,
    call_fn,
    open_timeout_seconds: int = 60,
):
    """Execute call_fn with circuit breaker protection."""
    state_data = get_circuit_state(service_name)
    state = state_data["state"]

    if state == CircuitState.OPEN.value:
        elapsed = time.time() - state_data["last_failure_time"]
        if elapsed < open_timeout_seconds:
            raise RuntimeError(f"Circuit OPEN for {service_name} — retry after {open_timeout_seconds - elapsed:.0f}s")
        # Transition to half-open for probe
        state = CircuitState.HALF_OPEN.value

    try:
        result = call_fn()
        record_success(service_name)
        return result
    except Exception as e:
        record_failure(service_name)
        logging.warning(f"Circuit failure for {service_name}: {e}")
        raise
Anti-patterns to Avoid
The Architecture Center documents common architecture mistakes that degrade performance, reliability, or cost. Understanding these prevents repeated mistakes when designing Azure workloads.
Anti-pattern
Symptom
Fix
Chatty I/O
Hundreds of small storage or database calls per request
Batch reads, use Cosmos DB transactional batch, cache frequently-read data in Redis
Noisy Neighbour
One service starves shared resources (CPU, IOPS, connections)
Isolate workloads with dedicated resources, use consumption-based services
Improper Instantiation
Re-creating expensive objects (HTTP clients, DB connections) per request
Use static/singleton clients; HttpClientFactory pattern in .NET; connection pool in Python
Synchronous Wait on I/O
Thread pool exhaustion under load
Use async/await throughout; never block async code with .Result or .Wait()
Retry Storm
All callers retry simultaneously after a transient failure
Add jitter to retry backoff; use circuit breaker to stop retries when downstream is down
Wrong tier for the workload
Paying for Premium when Consumption would work, or vice versa
Profile actual usage patterns before choosing SKU; use Azure Advisor cost recommendations
Detecting Chatty I/O with Application Insights

from azure.monitor.query import LogsQueryClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import datetime

def find_chatty_endpoints(
    workspace_id: str,
    threshold_calls_per_request: int = 20,
    hours_back: int = 24,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Query Application Insights for HTTP endpoints that generate many
    downstream calls per inbound request — a signal of chatty I/O.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    client = LogsQueryClient(credential)

    query = f"""
    requests
    | where timestamp > ago({hours_back}h)
    | where success == true
    | join kind=inner (
        dependencies
        | where timestamp > ago({hours_back}h)
        | summarize dep_count = count() by operation_Id
    ) on operation_Id
    | summarize
        avg_deps = avg(dep_count),
        p95_deps = percentile(dep_count, 95),
        call_count = count()
      by name
    | where avg_deps >= {threshold_calls_per_request}
    | order by avg_deps desc
    """

    end = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
    start = end - datetime.timedelta(hours=hours_back)
    result = client.query_workspace(workspace_id, query, timespan=(start, end))

    rows = []
    if result.tables:
        cols = [c.name for c in result.tables[0].columns]
        for row in result.tables[0].rows:
            d = dict(zip(cols, row))
            rows.append({
                "endpoint": d.get("name"),
                "avg_downstream_calls": round(d.get("avg_deps", 0), 1),
                "p95_downstream_calls": d.get("p95_deps"),
                "sample_count": d.get("call_count"),
            })
    return rows

chatty = find_chatty_endpoints("your-workspace-id")
print(f"Chatty endpoints ({len(chatty)}):")
for e in chatty:
    print(f"  {e['endpoint']}: avg {e['avg_downstream_calls']} downstream calls/request")
Well-Architected Framework Integration

The Architecture Center hosts the Azure Well-Architected Framework (WAF) — five pillars of architectural excellence that apply to every Azure workload. Each pillar has detailed guidance and a self-assessment tool that produces prioritised recommendations.
Reliability — design for failure; use availability zones, geo-redundancy, and health probes.
Security — apply least-privilege access, encrypt at rest and in transit, use Private Link to remove public endpoints.
Cost Optimisation — right-size resources, use reservations and savings plans, delete idle resources.
Operational Excellence — automate deployments, use Infrastructure as Code, implement structured observability.
Performance Efficiency — choose the right service tier, profile before optimising, use caching and CDN.

# Launch the Azure Well-Architected Review assessment
# The assessment asks ~60 questions across the 5 pillars and produces a report
# Access it at: aka.ms/wellarchitected/review

# After completing the assessment, Advisor surfaces WAF recommendations
az advisor recommendation list \
  --filter "category eq 'HighAvailability'" \
  --query "[?impact=='High'].{title:shortDescription.problem, resource:resourceMetadata.resourceId}" \
  --output table

# Export full WAF assessment results via the Advisor REST API
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub}/providers/Microsoft.Advisor/advisorScore?api-version=2023-01-01" \
  --query "value[].{pillar:name, score:properties.score, potential:properties.potentialScoreIncrease}" \
  --output table
Using the Architecture Center as a Design Starting Point

The most effective way to use the Architecture Center is as a starting point, not a finishing point. Pick the reference architecture closest to your workload, adapt it to your scale and constraints, then validate it against the WAF pillars.
Search the Architecture Center for your workload type (e.g. 'web application', 'data pipeline', 'microservices').
Download the reference architecture's Bicep or Terraform and read the component descriptions to understand each service's role.
Identify which design decisions are constraints for your workload (regulatory, budget, team skill set) and adapt accordingly.
Run an Azure Well-Architected Review assessment against your adapted design.
Implement Advisor recommendations produced by the assessment, prioritised by impact.
Re-run the WAF assessment quarterly to track improvement.
Implementing the Retry Pattern with Exponential Backoff and Jitter

The Retry pattern is one of the most frequently misimplemented patterns in Azure workloads. Naive retry (fixed delay, all callers retry in sync) creates retry storms. The Architecture Center's guidance specifies exponential backoff with jitter as the correct implementation.

import time, random, logging, functools
from typing import Callable, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")

def retry_with_backoff(
    max_attempts: int = 5,
    base_delay_seconds: float = 1.0,
    max_delay_seconds: float = 60.0,
    jitter: bool = True,
    retryable_exceptions: tuple = (Exception,),
):
    """
    Decorator: retry a function with exponential backoff and optional jitter.
    Implements the pattern described in the Azure Architecture Center.
    """
    def decorator(func: Callable[..., T]) -> Callable[..., T]:
        @functools.wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs) -> T:
            attempt = 0
            while attempt < max_attempts:
                try:
                    return func(*args, **kwargs)
                except retryable_exceptions as exc:
                    attempt += 1
                    if attempt >= max_attempts:
                        logging.error(f"{func.__name__} failed after {max_attempts} attempts: {exc}")
                        raise
                    delay = min(base_delay_seconds * (2 ** (attempt - 1)), max_delay_seconds)
                    if jitter:
                        delay = delay * (0.5 + random.random() * 0.5)  # ±50% jitter
                    logging.warning(f"{func.__name__} attempt {attempt} failed; retrying in {delay:.2f}s: {exc}")
                    time.sleep(delay)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

# Usage with Azure SDK calls
import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

@retry_with_backoff(
    max_attempts=5,
    base_delay_seconds=1.0,
    retryable_exceptions=(requests.exceptions.Timeout, requests.exceptions.ConnectionError),
)
def fetch_resource_state(resource_id: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch resource state with automatic retry on transient network errors."""
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = cred.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    url = f"https://management.azure.com{resource_id}?api-version=2021-04-01"
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

# Azure SDKs already implement retry internally, but you need this for
# raw requests calls or third-party API integrations
state = fetch_resource_state("/subscriptions/sub/resourceGroups/rg/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/api")
print(state.get("properties", {}).get("state"))
Implementing the Strangler Fig Pattern with API Management

The Strangler Fig pattern migrates a monolith incrementally — new functionality goes to microservices, old functionality stays in the monolith, and a routing layer (API Management) directs traffic. Over time the monolith is fully replaced without a big-bang rewrite.
API Management Routing Policy for Strangler Fig

<!-- APIM inbound policy: route /orders/* to new microservice, everything else to monolith -->
<policies>
  <inbound>
    <base />
    <choose>
      <when condition="@(context.Request.OriginalUrl.Path.StartsWith('/orders'))">
        <!-- New microservice on Container Apps -->
        <set-backend-service base-url="https://orders-api.internal.example.com" />
        <set-header name="X-Migration-Source" exists-action="override">
          <value>api-management</value>
        </set-header>
      </when>
      <otherwise>
        <!-- Legacy monolith -->
        <set-backend-service base-url="https://monolith.internal.example.com" />
      </otherwise>
    </choose>
  </inbound>
  <backend>
    <base />
  </backend>
  <outbound>
    <base />
  </outbound>
</policies>
Tracking Migration Progress

"""
Track Strangler Fig migration progress by measuring traffic split
between monolith and microservice backends via APIM diagnostic logs.
"""
from azure.monitor.query import LogsQueryClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import datetime

def migration_traffic_split(
    workspace_id: str,
    apim_name: str,
    days_back: int = 7,
) -> dict:
    """Return the % of requests already handled by microservices vs monolith."""
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    client = LogsQueryClient(cred)

    query = f"""
    AzureDiagnostics
    | where ResourceType == "APIMANAGEMENT/SERVICE"
    | where Resource =~ "{apim_name}"
    | where TimeGenerated > ago({days_back}d)
    | extend backend = extract("X-Migration-Source: (\\S+)", 1, requestHeaders_s)
    | summarize
        microservice = countif(backend == "api-management"),
        monolith = countif(backend != "api-management" or isnull(backend)),
        total = count()
      by bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
    | order by TimeGenerated asc
    """
    end = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
    start = end - datetime.timedelta(days=days_back)
    result = client.query_workspace(workspace_id, query, timespan=(start, end))

    daily = []
    if result.tables:
        for row in result.tables[0].rows:
            date, micro, mono, total = row[0], row[1], row[2], row[3]
            pct = round(micro / total * 100, 1) if total else 0
            daily.append({"date": str(date)[:10], "microservice_pct": pct, "total_requests": total})

    return {
        "apim": apim_name,
        "period_days": days_back,
        "daily_split": daily,
        "current_microservice_pct": daily[-1]["microservice_pct"] if daily else 0,
    }

split = migration_traffic_split("your-workspace-id", "my-apim")
print(f"Current microservice traffic: {split['current_microservice_pct']}%")
for d in split["daily_split"]:
    bar = "#" * int(d["microservice_pct"] / 5)
    print(f"  {d['date']}: {bar} {d['microservice_pct']}% ({d['total_requests']:,} requests)")
Workload-Specific Guidance

The Architecture Center provides specialised guidance for specific industry and workload types beyond the generic reference architectures. These workload guides cover unique requirements, compliance considerations, and service selection decisions.
Workload Guide
Key Differentiator
Notable Services
Mission-critical workloads
Zero-downtime deployments, chaos engineering, multi-region active-active
Availability Zones, Traffic Manager, Cosmos DB multi-region write
Financial services
PCI-DSS compliance, audit trails, encryption key management
Dedicated HSM, Key Vault Managed HSM, Policy, Defender
Healthcare
HIPAA BAA, PHI data residency, medical imaging storage
Azure Health Data Services, FHIR API, Compliance Manager
HPC (high-performance computing)
InfiniBand networking, GPU clusters, job scheduling
HPC Cache, CycleCloud, NDm A100 VMs, Azure Batch
IoT
Device provisioning, message ingestion, edge processing
IoT Hub, Device Provisioning Service, IoT Edge, Event Hubs
Generative AI
LLM hosting, RAG architectures, semantic caching
Azure OpenAI, AI Search, Prompt Flow, Content Safety
Workload-specific guides are the fastest path to compliance for regulated industries — they pre-answer 'is Azure compliant for X?' with concrete control mappings and deployment templates.
Keeping Architecture Decisions Documented

The Architecture Center promotes Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) as a way to document why a design choice was made — not just what was chosen. ADRs age well because they capture the context and constraints at decision time, making future reviews much faster.

# ADR-012: Use Azure Service Bus over Event Hubs for order events

## Status
Accepted — 2026-07-01

## Context
We need to route order events from the checkout service to three downstream consumers:
inventory, shipping, and analytics. Events need at-least-once delivery and
per-consumer independent replay capability.

## Decision
Use Azure Service Bus Topics with three subscriptions (inventory, shipping, analytics).

## Rationale
- Event Hubs is optimised for high-throughput streaming (millions/sec) but requires
  consumers to manage their own offset checkpointing.
- Service Bus Topics provide dead-letter queues, message TTL, deduplication, and
  independent per-subscription filters out of the box — matching our requirements.
- Order volume is < 10,000/min — well within Service Bus Standard tier limits.
- The retry and dead-letter semantics of Service Bus reduce custom error-handling code
  compared to an Event Hubs consumer group implementation.

## Consequences
- Cost: ~$10/mo on Standard tier vs. ~$0.20/mo for Event Hubs Basic.
  Accepted — the operational simplicity justifies the delta at this volume.
- If order volume grows beyond 1M/hr, revisit this decision: Event Hubs Premium
  would then be cheaper at scale.

## Alternatives Considered
- Azure Storage Queue: no topic/subscription fan-out; rejected.
- Event Grid: push-based, no dead-letter replay; rejected.
- Event Hubs: rejected as above — requires custom consumer offset management.
Summary

The Azure Architecture Center provides reference architectures, cloud design patterns, anti-patterns, Well-Architected Framework guidance, and best-practice articles — all produced and maintained by Azure engineering teams.
Reference architectures ship with deployable Bicep/Terraform code in companion GitHub repositories — clone and adapt rather than building from scratch.
24 cloud design patterns cover recurring distributed systems challenges; the most critical for Azure workloads are Circuit Breaker, Retry, Saga, and Competing Consumers.
Common anti-patterns — Chatty I/O, Improper Instantiation, Retry Storm — are detectable with Application Insights and addressable with architectural changes rather than infrastructure scaling.
The Well-Architected Framework assessment (aka.ms/wellarchitected/review) produces a prioritised improvement report across the five pillars; re-run it quarterly.
Use the Architecture Center as a starting point: pick the closest reference architecture, adapt to your constraints, validate with WAF, and implement Advisor recommendations.
